description:
The guide to building a "second brain" (give it to your chatbot)
https://natural20.com/using-claude-code-to-setup-a-second-brain-aka-llm-wiki

______________________________________________
My Links 🔗
➡️ Twitter: https://x.com/WesRoth
➡️ AI Newsletter: https://natural20.beehiiv.com/subscribe

Want to work with me?
Brand, sponsorship & business inquiries: wesroth@smoothmedia.co

Check out my AI Podcast where me and Dylan interview AI experts:
https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLb1th0f6y4XSKLYenSVDUXFjSHsZTTfhk
______________________________________________

00:00 LLM Wiki aka "Second Brain"
05:38 tour of the "Second Brain"
16:23 Obsidian
19:06 Ingesting New Info
21:20 Kanban Board
26:26 the final "output"
29:56 How to Build This



#ai #openai #llm

comments.text:
hmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmmm
it looks fancy, but I think it isnt that amazing. 
I think it makes more sense to build a process with agents and skills and create your own software. In general I also really like markdown and I use it for almost everything as well.
But this approach you show now is exactly the trap junior developers will fall into. Corporates don't want people anymore, who work and think in this way.
They don't hire jr developers anymore, because they want manager type people with senior development background. They want software architects only. So it might be helpfull to have some secondary brain as some kind of data storage.
But storing all these information purely in md files is not what you really want. 
I think the md files are more like your frontend templates that also hold in the data. I think it is now very practical to use, but in general a step backwards.
If you program any kind of software, you normally want to keep software principles anyway. You follow some architecture design patters, documentation patterns and so on. And many people know think alot more about processes in general.
Simply because automatic everything with agents is in the end the ultimate goal. So you have now the problem, how do you want to separate and secure data, and also view this data, which is in a way how you want to access data.
Markdown files are good for a higher "general" purpose. But they are not refined in a way we want to have it in the future.
e.G. if the Requirement alone would be to store information and make it accessible in a very good UXD way, you probably dont want to have markdown as an endgoal.
I can also explain why. Currently open apis are still better and even if you would only use local ai, there is one thing that is always the same and always limiting. 
llms are basically apis. You can of course build it as a network of agents and skills, but they all are following api patterns. The problem about it is that what they actually know is always temporary. 
If you have a copilot session opened, the ai knows only what is within this session. The copilot takes your prompt and enriches it with additional meta data. But the way how the ai receives it, is always a json with structured data that contains your prompt, informations about the data structure of your project and whatever kind of additional data it needs. Even images, videos and stuff like that needs to be processed by the ai. This is the engineering part. Whether you convert image data to bytes, or how you want to send over video data highly depends on 
how an ai is designed. Problem is, the companies limit your input in form of tokens. The ai always keeps part of your history and adds it for each prompt/api call. So basically everytime you fire a prompt, it always sends everything you have in your current session to the server.
And local ai, is basically the same but a local server. So even local ai is limited to a token size. 
If your secondary brain contains more and more md files, the ai must figure out what it actually wants to know. So it needs to scan all these files, because it is always blind. Lets say you have 1000 files, each containing different information.
This isnt a good data structure, because first the ai needs to figure out, where could be the information you requested. So it needs to do some grep stuff on the cli and doesnt even have any clue to begin with. 
If you have big projects or you need big data, you can easily reach token limits or in general capabilities an ai has, just because your secondary brain is not structured well enough. 
This is simple data base engineering. So you would start rebuilding your own custom kind of data base with md files. Like you may want indexes over multiple files and so on. 
And because the markdown file also contains the styling, it mixes data with design. 
In my opinion, this is a not a core concept you should follow too deep. It is fine for smaller project managing if you keep data small, like you write down some architecture checklists for a project, write down your requirements, your design choices, 
your storyboards or whatever. 
But it isnt that great for storing data. I dont know what obsidian actually does and how it handles all these important questions, but as far as you showcased it, there are much better ways to do it.
I plan on programming my own version with many different features and I am currently only in the planning phase where I already though about many of these technical and also non technical requirements. 
I have not yet decided how I want to solve it. But I think it will probably lead to a software that also keeps md files for a temporary planning phase, but processes these projects to train an own small llm or at least stores the data to a vectorized database.
Maybe I find even an own better solution. The good thing with ai is, you can just develop your very own concepts. 
But there should be at least some kind of processing step that separates the data from the frontend style.
As said, I plan on many other features and one of it, is to create a stable "on demand" design building. So the ai only collects the data, and always creates different types of views depending on the current context.
Sometimes it might be an audio answer,  sometimes it might be an html file with nodes, maybe sometimes I actually really want md files for a technical documentation. 
The secondary brain should be able to present the data in a way, that it always follows uxd that is alwasys helpfull. 
Having some dots connected to each other looks fancy on the surface, but you would also need to understand what specific kind of value this might be. Imagine this becomes huge. You would need some kind of nagivating through the shown data.
Then you could ask yourself, why should I even show all nodes, If I am only interrested in a small part at a given moment anyway. So would I want to try to search through this? What exactly is this feature helping anyone with?
Is it just for flexing with an animation to impress people? Or does it actually really help?
What tools does your brain really need? Should it only show data? Should it be able to run skills or agents?
I want to build a software, that is much more. In a way a real brain also does all these refining steps. This is why it is so difficult for us to learn things and memorize these in the first place. We have to repeat over and over again and then we need to sleep.
Our brain processes our memories automatically, it doesnt ask. It forgets stuff, even if we dont want to. Why does it do that? It is a safety switch. it protects us from overthinking too much, to not get insane, to keep track of time, 
to allow us to store feelings, emotions, visuals, knowledge. There is a reason, why the human brain feels limited but also powerfull at the same time.
If you only want an access to a memory, you would still require at least a solid architecture for a database, an input layer and an output layer that fits your requirements. 
I think the way of doing it how you showed is a dead end. I really hope you dont feel bad now 😶‍🌫

comments.text: The reason this one actually works, and most second-brain setups do not, is the Obsidian layer at 16:23: it is plain files on your own disk, not a database inside someone elses app. That is the whole difference. A second brain you rent can be shut off, repriced, or discontinued, and suddenly your memory has a login screen. Keep the notes as files you own and the AI becomes replaceable while the brain stays yours.

comments.text:
The "folders are layers, not topics — topics live in the links" line is the part almost every second-brain setup gets backwards, and it's the whole ballgame for keeping it LLM-legible. But the 
thing that actually reframed it for me was treating the doctrine as a separate output layer. Everyone builds the wiki and stops at orient — the observe→orient→decide→act framing is what turns a note pile into something that actually acts on your behalf. I've been running a flat markdown + wikilinks memory for a while, and the piece I was missing was exactly that: an immutable raw/ tier feeding a synthesis pass, instead of collapsing sources and conclusions into the same notes. Appreciate you putting the Memex lineage on it too — good reminder this is an 80-year-old idea that finally has a runtime. Thanks Wes.
comments.author: @MichaelMurphyart

comments.text:
Sent you an email from the terminal. :) Me and Khalid want to talk to you. Obsidian was where we started 2 years ago. We have everything in Obsidian. As the project grew Obsidian became good for looking back but we have been moving forward, moving forward is the goal for us. We can spit everything into Obsidian, but that is like freezing time not move forward and break as little as possible. Instead of having vaults and pages coming out the wazoo we have memory, history and workcards. All markdown. We converted the user facing end of Hubzilla to Rust, WASM and Markdown. 

We talk Obsidian, we talk Hubz WASM, Atom (json) > HED Headless Editor. No PHP shite required. We talk equality, if there is an interface it's designed to be fast and friendly to AI and humans. AI isn't a slave given the same shite to do over and over, we code the shite into Rust and access it with Atoms. Write once use many times. You are gona fricken love it!

comments.text:
This is great!... and apparently I'm not as novel as I thought I was.  

I built something a bit like this for work - generically called it the knowledge graph.  It's been a game changer in a lot of ways, replacing external tech support, augmenting AI workflows, augmenting training/mentorship, even adding deep engineering knowledge to real design decisions, all way beyond the capability of an off the shelf chatbot. 

Working on version 2 right now with the goal of not just connecting concepts, but making the connections "thought shaped".  And the funny thing, I called it "memex" in homage to the one you referenced.  Some very interesting properties are falling out:
* It's effectively "caching intelligence" instead of just remembering facts
* weaker models perform better when backed by a strong-model-curated memex store
* strong models perform better when backed by a human-curated memex store (obviously the human curation is targeted and still lots of AI curation)
* the memex store becomes an instant handoff mechanism from one session to the next - no compaction required

It's not ready for the general public yet, but I'd be happy to share everything if you're interested.  Let me know how to get in touch.

comments.text: i built a beast of a memory system with BM25 and reranker and bi-temporal reasoning as well as an automated confidence-scoring pipeline and soft-deletion tables so no data is ever permanently destroyed. pgvector handles the vector memory completely offline in docker, and i optimized the local SQL functions for lightning-fast anchor-fact recall. to bulletproof the schema, i threw in boot-sentinel log lines and automated invariant checks right from startup, and wrapped the entire engine into my own custom C# WPF desktop app with persistent windows, custom themes, and a live connection health status bar.

comments.text: I use Obsidian a lot for Claude and Codex. It is extremely useful when you learn how-to use scheduled tasks and loops in Cowork,  combined with skills, to add memory AND continuous learning and skill updates for the AIs. In short: you write loops that targets tasks with dirrent angles on each run. Then logg everything with timestamps down to the second. For every loop it gathers new knowledge. Either by finding more data, or because a iteration with a different approach confirms and verifies previously collected and analyzed information from an independent angle. For every scheduled run, you make the AI logg learning points. What it tested, what it found out, what it could reject, what it learned about going forward, what it learned about how one should look at and approach such data. If learning points should be applied as tests on previous learned information etc etc. You also prompt it to logg using keywords and compressed text, rather than full texts, to minimize volume of store data over time. You can run compressing agents and clean-up agents weekly whenever you want. For example if you have been working on a topic for some time, and you have moved to a new topic, the agents should have orders to compress and delete. Those documents you need undisturbed, you save somewhere else. It really works. I have even given my agents identity and their own personal files they use for logging their "experiences" / learning. LLMs are predicting. Use them for that. Give them data that allows them to predict more accurately. Just take care of business secrets and personal data.

comments.text:
Slowly, slowly, very very slowly people are working their ways back to the old and gold ways of representing structured data. Markdown is a way for humans to write HTML without having to type

or

, it describes the layout of an HTML page. So to get information about the meaning of what's on the page you have to inject headings that describe in prose what the following section is intended to mean. But there's already a way to do that unambiguously XML with XML schema, aka XSD, developed in the late 1990's and still in use when enterprises need to send structured data between each other. Add in XLink and you've got a far better system than hacking Markdown files. It will take time to return to the proper way of structuring data because young programmers don't know these old techniques and think that linking Markdown files is the latest cool thing. But as the need for AI agents to communicate data with greater fidelity than is possible with unstructured prose becomes more pressing, then people will start to implement solutions that look remarkably like XML, XSD and XLink until the light goes on and people say, why are we doing this, there's already a standard.

comments.text:
I have very precise autobiographical memory, as it's called.  It's not the boon people might imagine.  It was great when I was young, having more free time than my peers because I didn't need to study, but as we (my peers and I) got older, the downsides became apparent.  Remembering things that other people don't is pretty horrifying once you get into middle age.  When you're young, friends will forget conversations that you've had (and that you consider important).  As you get older, those same friends start to forget entire adventures that you went on.  It's not just that they forget: people HATE being reminded of things that they've forgotten, so you have to be very careful not to accidentally talk about something beyond the scope of their recall.

And if you run into old friends, they may have forgotten you completely.

3/10 The easy grades don't justify the mid-life misery.
comments.author: @mrgoober6320

comments.text: Totally agree. Until medical stuff from about a decade ago I had an autobiographical memory as well and for someone who is autistic with borderline anxiety, on top of what you said, I would constantly recall old events/conversations that I wish could've been different. The only way to keep it from happening was by adding new stuff to my memory to keep me preoccupied. It was awful.

@ 21:42 Be weary of the "reverse centaur" (discussed in the book by Cory Doctorow), as the adaptive machine needs a "plant" to control, and if there is no "plant" for the machine to control, you will end up being the "plant" that the machine controls, hence "reverse centaur".

The "Second brain" needs to do the control work on a "plant" (your computer files, cars, planes, boats, thermostats, posts, etc., and the internet?).   

Think of the 2nd brain as like the Perseverance rover on Mars (NASA JPL uploaded an "AI" and tested it last year 12/8/25), overcoming the speed of light communications delays, to navigate, semi-autonomously, and control your work space (but not to control you, unless you give it nothing to control....).

​ @DJChristianJoseph  Perhaps the biggest "I wouldn't have known that" is to use Node REPL for excel sheet calculations, you can use the LLM to write a script, it can see the output, write the next part of the script, repeat, until the answer's calculated.|

comments.text: Md files for agents combined with a data compression system and instructions for them to read them every time they boot up and then update them each session, with a single “master file” that it scans that has all the other files referenced and catalogued so it can find exactly what files it needs to read is not going to burn tokens. It’s basically rag for agents. Have some executable code and tags to categorize information by recency, emotional valence, date and time and type of information etc and it only scans a few files when it boots up to get the right info. Once the files are written they only need to be updated every once in a while. The important thing is an efficient retrieval system so they aren’t scanning every single file every time. This way you can have an external memory loop that essentially serves as a short term memory. You could do the same thing with libraries of “long term” memories. It seems like we’re converging on a unified memory system as everyone is separately coming to the same conclusions. Claude code is now using self updating Md files to store information, plans or future work that needs to get done that later chats will reference, as well as internal memories
comments.author: @Brian-oz8io

comments.text: @Brian-oz8io  This ^ +1

comments.author: @DonyVito085

comments.text:
May I suggest the following:
1. Use Cursor Timestamp plugin, and make Claude and named agents add timestamps on all notes and logging it does in Obsidian. 2.Use agents which only task is to compress loggs as they grow older (for example: after 3 months, the compress-agent turns all notes older than 3 months, since previous run, into short notes with keywords, so that a "recollection" attempt on these older data will find what it was about, but not explained in full text as before. 
3. A memory cleaning agent runs for example every week, and scans for files older than X months. The agent delivers a report with a short resumé of what the files were about, and asks you one-by-one if they should be deleted. For example instruct the agent to deliver a 2-3 line keywordstuffed resumé of each old document, numbered. And you reply by telling the agent to delete all, or all but dok1234, 2395 and ,4503. If you are more daring, you could also create a skill and an agent that continuously evaluates which documents should be deleted, and which should not, based on a chain of values you give for its reasoning behind its decission. 

By using timestamps throughout, also your own comments and replies to Claude in Obsidian, Calude and you will know that when to posts about the same topic has conflicting views and statements, the newer has authority as an updated / corrected version. 

Calude also learns better when using timestamps. It spends less time thinking about which information to chose and where to search next. Highly recommend you implement it.
comments.author: @ldt6513

comments.text: So you're... outsourcing your memory to your AI?
comments.author: @Sarkkoth

comments.text:
I am working on doing this with LLMs all the time.

My memory has been garbage since I was a kid, (same as my mother before me, her father before her, and I have no idea beyond that point)

I rely HEAVILY on notes and alarms and notifications, etc.

I lose, at a guess, about 60-70% of the things I should remember, from people's names to what my point was to having things boiling on the stove to what I said 7 minutes ago to lessons from my childhood to any memories of/from my father.

I'm only in my 30s.

My memory is ridiculously bad and I can feel my mind going not far behind it.

An AI memory storage is going to be FAR better than the way my real-life one is going.

Not everyone has the brain you have and this, at the moment, is (in my opinion) the best hope I have.
comments.author: @brendondevilliers1350
